rxn_class_summary: Iron-catalyzed electrochemical nitration of alkenes, alkynes, aryl boronic acids and arenes (including halo-, decarboxylative, and deborylative variants) using Fe(NO3)3 as both NO2-radical source and redox mediator
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Fe(III) to Fe(II) initiates the cycle; Fe(II) reduces nitrate (or Fe(III)-bound nitrate) to release NO2 radical; the Fe(II) generated is re-oxidized at the anode (along with halide A- to A2) to regenerate Fe(III)

key_intermediates.name: [FeIII-X] halide complex for ligand transfer (X = Br, Cl)

electron_flow: Cathode injects an electron into Fe(III) to give Fe(II), which reduces nitrate to NO2 radical; NO2• adds to the alkene/alkyne or aryl pi-system to give a C-centered radical that is intercepted by Fe(III)-X ligand transfer (halonitration), by loss of CO2/Bpin/H+ (decarboxylative/deborylative/aromatic nitration); at the anode, halide is oxidized to X2 and Fe(II) reoxidized to Fe(III), closing the cycle.
rate_determining_step: Generation/release of NO2 radical from Fe(III)-nitrate via Fe(II) (electron injection-dependent), consistent with on/off current induction behavior

probe_reagents.name: 1-chloro-4-(1-cyclopropylvinyl)benzene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened bromo-nitroalkene product 4 isolated in 77% (E:Z = 1.1:1), confirming a carbon-centered radical intermediate after NO2• addition

probe_reagents.name: Current on/off
probe_reagents.observation: Conversion proceeds only while current is applied; no dark propagation, indicating continuous electrochemical initiation/turnover of Fe and induction period at low F/mol

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Invoked in decarboxylative mechanism as trapping the α-nitro-β-carboxylate radical, supporting radical intermediate

notes: Mechanism is unified across four substrate classes (alkenes, vinyl acids, vinyl boronates, arenes/ArB(OH)2) with the same Fe(III)/Fe(II) electrocatalytic cycle releasing NO2•. For halonitration, the C-radical is captured by Fe(III)-X (radical ligand transfer) and then undergoes base-mediated E2/intramolecular SN2 to give nitroalkenes or nitrolactones (81 & 82). For aryl nitration the cyclohexadienyl radical is oxidized by Fe(III) to a Wheland cation that loses H+ or B(OH)2 (ipso). An alternative direct anodic oxidation of nitrate to NO2• cannot be fully excluded but the Fe-mediated cathodic-initiation pathway is favored.
